data.title: From fragmented regulation to systematic governance: interpreting the EU MiCAR regulatory framework
data.description:
1. Introduction: From Regulatory Gaps to a Unified Framework In 2023, the European Union officially launched the landmark Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCAR). Against the backdrop of the gradual maturity of global crypto-asset regulation, the EU introduced the MiCAR regulatory framework, aiming to establish a unified crypto-asset regulatory framework across the 27 EU member states, replacing the previously fragmented regulatory practices across each member state.

According to the EU legislative process, MiCAR will be implemented in stages:

·From June 30, 2024, the key provisions related to stablecoins (ART and EMT parts) will be officially applicable;

·From December 30, 2024, the remaining provisions on crypto asset service provider (CASP) licensing, market manipulation prevention, investor protection, etc. will take full effect.

The background of MiCAR can be traced back to the "Digital Finance Strategy" proposed by the European Union in 2020. The core goal of this strategy is to balance innovation and regulation, protect investors and maintain financial stability. Under this framework, MiCAR, together with regulations such as the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA), constitute the core regulatory system for digital finance in the EU. More importantly, MiCAR is not just a "risk prevention" regulation, but the EU hopes to provide legal certainty for the long-term sustainable development of the blockchain and crypto industries through technology-neutral legislation, making it more practical. Below, this article will interpret the main contents of the MiCAR framework, such as the definition of crypto assets and asset reference tokens, and analyze the impact of this framework on the European crypto market.

2. Main contents of the MiCAR regulatory framework

MiCAR's regulatory system can be divided into two levels: crypto-assets and crypto-asset service providers.

2.1 Definition and classification of crypto-assets

On a technology-neutral basis, MiCAR defines "crypto-asset" as "a digital representation of value or rights that can be transferred and stored electronically through distributed ledger technology (DLT) or similar technologies." It divides crypto assets into three core categories, as follows: 2.1.1 Asset-Referenced Token (ART) ART is a cryptocurrency, distinct from an electronic money token (EMT), whose value is stabilized by reference to some other value, equity, or a combination thereof (Article 3, paragraph 1, item 6 of the MiCAR). Pursuant to Articles 16 and 20 of the MiCAR, the entity proposing to issue an ART must complete an authorization procedure prior to issuance, and the issuer must be a legal person or authorized entity established in the EU. The authorization procedure must be initiated through a formal application (Article 18 of the MiCAR). In addition, the application must include a legal opinion confirming that the cryptocurrency exists, falls within the scope of the MiCAR definition, and is not an electronic money token (EMT). Finally, the proposed issuer must submit a white paper for the cryptocurrency, which can only be issued after approval. 2.1.2 Electronic Money Token (EMT, similar to a stablecoin) The value of an electronic money token is intended to be maintained by pegging it to the value of an official currency. These tokens can be considered stablecoins pegged to a single official currency (such as the euro or the US dollar). They are specifically defined in MiCAR and subject to specific regulation. According to Article 81, paragraph 1 of MiCAR, only credit institutions or electronic money institutions may issue electronic money tokens (EMTs). Furthermore, since EMTs are legally classified as electronic money, issuers must comply with Chapters II and III of the Electronic Money Directive (EMD). MiCAR does not prescribe an authorization procedure for EMT issuers; they only need to notify the authorities and publish a white paper. 2.1.3 Other Cryptocurrencies These cryptocurrencies, such as utility tokens and Bitcoin, are neither asset reference tokens (ARTs) nor electronic money tokens (EMTs), and are not excluded from MiCAR. Therefore, they generally do not require an issuance license. In principle, these cryptocurrencies still require the preparation of a white paper, notification to the authorities, and public publication, but exemptions exist if certain conditions are met. 2.2 Crypto-Asset Service Provider (CASP) System MiCAR has established for the first time a unified regulatory system for crypto-asset service providers, proposing systematic regulatory requirements for crypto-asset service providers (CASPs), covering services such as custody, trading, exchange, consulting, issuance, and transfer. The core requirements for CASPs include: 2.2.1 Unified Licensing System (passporting): Once a CASP obtains a MiCAR license in any member state, it can operate throughout the EU; this is known as the EU passporting mechanism. The core of MiCAR is to bring all companies providing cryptoasset services to EU users under the unified regulatory framework of CASPs. Any CASP wishing to operate within the EU must obtain authorization in any member state. They can then serve the entire EU market through the "single license" principle. Furthermore, MiCAR specifies 10 categories of service activities. Any company engaging in any of these activities within the EU must obtain a MiCAR license and be subject to regulatory constraints.

This classification system covers almost all major business forms in today's crypto market. This also means that whether it is a mature large-scale trading platform or an innovative project in the early stages, as long as it provides relevant services to EU users, it must be included in the MiCAR supervision scope. 2.2.2 Transitional Arrangements: To ensure a smooth transition, MiCAR establishes transitional provisions: CASPs that have been operating in compliance with national laws before December 30, 2024, will be allowed to continue operating during the transition period until they obtain or are denied a MiCAR license, or cease operations on July 1, 2026 at the latest. Member States may set their own transition periods, the length of which varies from country to country. This arrangement provides the market with an 18-month buffer period, allowing regulators and industry players ample time to conduct institutional integration and compliance adjustments. It also effectively resolves the previous problem of "multiple regulation" within the EU, making the regulatory environment more certain and competitively fair.

3. Impact on the crypto tax regulatory landscape

The introduction of MiCAR is not only an update to the regulatory system, but also has a profound impact on the EU's tax policy and compliance regulatory landscape. 3.1 Issuance Supervision: From White Paper Disclosure to Reserve Constraints 3.1.1 Ordinary Cryptoasset Issuance: White Paper Disclosure + Light Regulation Under the MiCAR system, for ordinary cryptoassets that do not fall under ART or EMT, supervision adopts a milder approach of "disclosure first, approval second." First, the issuer must be a company or legal entity with legal entity status so that its actions can be legally traceable and accountable, and even in the event of disputes, accountability can be pursued. Secondly, the issuer must draft and publish a crypto-asset white paper in accordance with MiCAR requirements, disclosing key information, including but not limited to: the issuer's name, registered address, and governance structure; the technical architecture, working principles, and equity mechanism of the issued token; risk disclosures (such as smart contract risks, liquidity risks, and policy risks); investor rights and obligations, fee structure, and issuance/burning mechanisms; and compliance statements (e.g., phrases such as "This white paper has not been approved by EU competent authorities" to avoid misleading investors into believing it is officially endorsed). Furthermore, MiCAR requires issuers to implement an ongoing update obligation regarding significant changes. This means that if changes occur in project structure, funding arrangements, risk factors, and other factors that may affect investment decisions, the white paper should be revised promptly or a revised explanation should be disclosed to ensure investors always have the latest and most accurate information.

Under this mechanism, projects do not need to go through complicated pre-approval procedures, thereby lowering the entry threshold and facilitating the participation of innovators and small projects in the market. At the same time, through the design of information disclosure and accountability mechanisms, it can also balance the protection of investors' right to know and the maintenance of market vitality.

3.1.2 Stablecoins: Strong Regulation + Rigid Constraints on Reserves

Unlike the relatively relaxed issuance system mentioned above, MiCAR imposes a strict and rigid regulatory framework on the issuance of stablecoins, namely ART and EMT, to ensure the robustness of these tokens in terms of redemption, reserves, and security.

（1） Authorization requirements and white paper approval

From June 30, 2024, all projects that publicly issue ART or EMT in the EU, or list on exchanges, must be authorized by the competent authorities of the country where they are located.

In the case of ART, issuers other than credit institutions must apply for MiCAR authorization and must submit a white paper during the authorization process, which can only be issued after review by the competent authorities.

For EMT, the issuer must be a credit institution or an Electronic Money Institution (EMI), authorized in accordance with the traditional Electronic Money Directive (EMD) or other regulatory frameworks.

After the white paper is submitted, the competent authority must determine its completeness and compliance with regulatory requirements within a specified time period; if it does, it will approve or file it.

MiCAR also recognizes that some ARTs or EMTs may be large due to their size and other factors, and thus may pose higher risks. Therefore, the European Banking Authority (EBA) will assume supervisory responsibility for the issuance functions of institutions that issue important ARTs and some important EMTs under MiCAR.

（2） Segregation of Reserves and Assets

The segregation of reserves and assets is the most critical part of MiCAR’s regulatory design: the issuer must establish a pool of reserve assets that is isolated from its other assets and is used first to guarantee the redemption requests of token holders. In other words, even if the issuer goes bankrupt, this part of the reserve assets should not be used to repay debts or liquidate to other creditors.

The requirements for the composition and liquidity of reserves are also very strict:

· Reserves must be diversified and can only include highly liquid, low-risk assets (such as deposits, government bonds, high-quality covered bonds, certain money market instruments, etc.). Regarding the deposit ratio with credit institutions, the EBA's draft Regulatory Technical Standard (RTS) published in 2024 recommends that at least 30% of the funds of non-significant stablecoins be deposited in banks to ensure basic redemption capabilities. If the stablecoin is deemed significant, a 60% deposit is required. Furthermore, the issuer should be able to promptly liquidate reserve assets when token holders request redemption. (Please refer to the original text of RTS: Article 36(1) of Regulation (EU) 2023/1114 requires issuers of asset-referenced tokens (ARTs), whether they are either if the ARTs are significant ARTs or not, to constitute and maintain a reserve of assets at all times to cover their liabilities against the holders of their issued ARTs matching the risks reflected within these liabilities. The reserve of assets is composed of the assets received when issuing the token holders and by the highly liquid financial instruments the issuer may invest in. In the case of tokens referenced to official currencies, a minimum part of the reserves should be held in the form of deposits in credit institutions (at least 30% of the amount referenced in each official currency if the token is not significant, and at least 60% if the token is significant). Upon redemption requests from token holders, the issuers should be able to liquidate the reserve assets.)

4. Recommendations for Investors and Institutions

Faced with the systemic regulatory shift brought about by MiCAR, European investors and crypto companies should adopt proactive compliance and risk management strategies.

4.1 Investor Level: Strengthening Tax Compliance and Filing

Systemic regulatory shifts have created a need for automated tax compliance tools. For institutional investors with larger business volumes and more complex structures, relying solely on personalized tools is insufficient to meet compliance and audit requirements. Individual investors can also use such tools to record transaction and income data in real time, enabling automated tax filing and reporting, improving efficiency and accuracy. Take FinTax Suite, for example. This modular system seamlessly integrates with mainstream ERP systems. Through its intelligent rules engine and multi-dimensional reporting system, it covers key aspects such as data capture, automated accounting, report generation, and compliance audits, helping companies achieve financial transparency and tax compliance within a global regulatory environment. FinTax Suite also supports audit-ready GAAP/IFRS-compliant financial reporting, a dual-account system for stablecoins and fiat currencies, AI-OCR invoice recognition, and bank statement import, providing a comprehensive financial and tax management solution for on-chain payment and high-frequency trading companies. Furthermore, multinational investors should pay attention to the cross-border reporting requirements under DAC8, which clarify the differences in capital gains tax and VAT among EU member states. 4.2 Institutional Level: Preparing for MiCAR License Applications in Advance For crypto exchanges, custodians, and wallet providers, obtaining MiCAR authorization is a prerequisite for entering the EU market. Institutions planning to enter the European market should communicate with EU member state regulators in advance to clarify the length of their respective transition periods. While MiCAR authorization may be a high barrier to entry, obtaining it grants access to the entire EU market, which can provide a significant competitive advantage in the long term. Third-country companies wishing to provide cryptoasset services in the EU must also establish an entity within the EU and apply for CASP authorization in accordance with MiCAR. The only exception is so-called "reverse solicitation," where clients initiate service requests entirely on their own initiative. It's important to note that the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA)'s final report on reverse solicitation aims to tighten the scope of reverse solicitation under the MiCAR framework. Non-EU platforms that reach EU clients through reverse solicitation without authorization may expose investors to legal risks. 5. Conclusion: MiCAR: Balancing Regulation and Innovation The introduction of the EU's MiCAR marks the official transition of crypto assets from their wild growth phase to a more mature and regulated mainstream financial system in Europe. It both addresses risks and provides an institutional foundation for innovation. In the coming years, the integration of MiCAR with regulations such as DAC8 and DORA will foster a more transparent, secure, and efficient crypto market. For investors, compliance is no longer a burden, but a safeguard for legitimate and long-term returns. For businesses, while MiCAR imposes a threshold, it also serves as a passport to entry into one of the world's largest crypto markets. For all market participants, the implementation of MiCAR is not only a comprehensive compliance test, but also a critical window to seize opportunities and achieve business growth. Only by proactively adapting to regulatory trends and deeply integrating compliance concepts into corporate strategy and operations can we remain invincible in the new competitive landscape.

data.title: Tiger Research Report: Bitcoin Valuation to $200,000 in Q4 2025
data.description:
Author: Daniel Kim, Ryan Yoon, Jay Jo; Source: Tiger Research; Compiler: Shaw Golden Finance

This report presents Tiger Research’s outlook for Bitcoin in the fourth quarter of 2025, with a target of $200,000, citing continued buying by institutional investors amid market volatility, the Federal Reserve’s rate cuts, and the October plunge that confirmed institutional dominance in the market.

Key Points

  • Institutional investors continued to increase their holdings amid volatility - ETF net inflows remained stable in the third quarter, and MSTR increased its holdings by 388 bitcoins in a single month, strengthening its belief in long-term investment;

  • Overheated but not yet extreme - The MVRV-Z index was 2.31, indicating that valuations were high but not yet extreme. The liquidation of leveraged funds cleared out short-term traders, creating room for the next wave of increases;

  • The global liquidity environment continued to improve - The broad money supply (M2) exceeded US$96 trillion, a record high. Expectations of a Fed rate cut have increased, and it is expected that there will be 1-2 more rate cuts this year. Institutional investors bought amidst Sino-US trade uncertainty. In the third quarter of 2025, the Bitcoin market slowed from its strong second-quarter gains (up 28% month-over-month) and entered a period of volatile sideways trading (up 1% month-over-month). On October 6, Bitcoin hit a record high of $126,210. However, the Trump administration's renewed trade pressure on China caused the price of Bitcoin to pull back 18% to $104,000, significantly increasing volatility. According to Volmex Finance's Bitcoin Volatility Index (BVIV), Bitcoin volatility narrowed from March to September as institutional investors steadily increased their holdings. However, it surged 41% after September, exacerbating market uncertainty (Chart 1). This pullback appears temporary, driven by renewed Sino-US trade friction and Trump's hawkish rhetoric. Institutional strategic buying, led by Strategy Inc. (MSTR), is actually accelerating. The macroeconomic environment also contributed to the market's upward trend. Global broad money supply (M2) exceeded $96 trillion, a record high, while the Federal Reserve cut interest rates by 25 basis points to a range of 4.00%-4.25% on September 17. The Fed hinted at one or two more rate cuts this year. A stable labor market and a recovering economy are creating favorable conditions for risky assets. Institutional inflows remained strong. Net inflows into Bitcoin spot ETFs reached $7.8 billion in the third quarter. While lower than the $12.4 billion in Q2, net inflows throughout Q3 confirm steady buying from institutional investors. This momentum continued into Q4, with $3.2 billion recorded in the first week of October alone, a record high for weekly inflows in 2025. This suggests that institutional investors view price corrections as strategic entry opportunities. Strategy continued buying during the market correction, purchasing 220 BTC on October 13 and 168 BTC on October 20, for a total of 388 BTC in one week. This demonstrates that institutional investors remain steadfast in their belief in Bitcoin's long-term value, regardless of short-term volatility. On-chain data signals overheating, but fundamentals remain unchanged. On-chain analysis reveals some signs of overheating, though valuations are not yet worrisome. The MVRV-Z indicator (market capitalization to realized value ratio) is currently in overheated territory at 2.31, but has stabilized relative to the extreme valuation range approached in July and August (Exhibit 2). The net unrealized profit and loss ratio (NUPL) also showed overheating territory, but has eased from the high unrealized profit situation in the second quarter (Figure 3). The adjusted expenditure-output profit ratio (aSOPR), which reflects investors' realized profits and losses, is very close to its equilibrium value of 1.03, suggesting no cause for concern (Chart 4). Bitcoin's transaction count and active user count remained at similar levels compared to the previous quarter, suggesting a temporary slowdown in network growth (Chart 5). Meanwhile, total transaction volume is trending upward. A decrease in the number of transactions coupled with an increase in volume suggests that larger amounts of funds are being moved in fewer transactions, suggesting an increase in large-scale capital flows. However, we cannot simply interpret the increase in trading volume as a positive sign. Recent increases in funds flowing into centralized exchanges generally indicate that holders are preparing to sell (Chart 6). In the absence of improvements in fundamental indicators such as transaction counts and active users, the increase in trading volume is more indicative of short-term capital flows and selling pressure amidst high volatility, rather than genuine demand. The October 11th Crash Demonstrates a Shift in Market Dominance from Institutional to Institutional. The October 11th crash on centralized exchanges (a 14% drop) demonstrates the Bitcoin market's shift from retail to institutional dominance. The key point is this: the market reaction is radically different from previous ones. In a similar environment at the end of 2021, panic spread among retail investors, leading to a subsequent crash. This time, the pullback was limited. Following a large-scale liquidation, institutional investors continued to buy, demonstrating their resolute defense of the market's downside potential. Furthermore, institutions appear to view this as a healthy consolidation that will help eliminate excessive speculative demand. In the short term, the series of sell-offs will lower the average purchase price for retail investors and increase psychological pressure, potentially exacerbating volatility due to dampened market sentiment. However, if institutional investors continue to enter the market during this period of sideways trading, this pullback could lay the foundation for the next leg of gains. Target Price Raised to $200,000 Using our TVM methodology for Q3 analysis, we arrive at a neutral base price of $154,000, a 14% increase from $135,000 in Q2. To this end, we apply a -2% fundamental adjustment and a +35% macro adjustment, arriving at a target price of $200,000. The -2% fundamental adjustment reflects a temporary slowdown in network activity and increased deposits on centralized exchanges, indicating short-term weakness. The macro adjustment remains at 35%. Continued global liquidity expansion and institutional inflows, along with the Federal Reserve's rate cuts, provide strong catalysts for a fourth-quarter rally. A short-term pullback may stem from signs of overheating, but this represents a healthy correction rather than a trend or shift in market perception. The continued rise in the base price indicates a steady increase in Bitcoin's intrinsic value. Despite the temporary weakness, the medium- to long-term upside outlook remains solid.

data.title: CZ Crushes Peter Schiff’s Dream of Tokenizing Gold on the Blockchain With One Tweet
data.description:
Peter Schiff Calls For A Return Back To Gold As He Launches Tokenized Gold

In one of the most ironic statements made this year, long-time Bitcoin critic Peter Schiff has just started a campaign to draw people to tokenise gold at a time when gold is experiencing a 25% trillion plunge in its value this week.

In his interview on the ThreadGuy podcast, Schiff announced his plans to launch his own tokenized gold product, where he claimed users would be able to buy and store gold in a vault via an app. Users would also be able to transfer ownership through a blockchain, or redeem it for physical gold.

Peter Schiff reveals he will be launching a tokenized gold product

"You'll be able to buy gold on an app through your phone, the gold will be stored in a vault and then you will be able to effortlessly transfer ownership of gold to people you know or redeem it for physical gold" pic.twitter.com/mWCXVKj9v8

— Tengen (@Crypto_Tengen) October 23, 2025

Schiff used the imminent fall of bitcoin for why people should be jumping on the gold bandwagon, stressing that Bitcoin has no intrinsic value and will eventually "go to zero." He also called Bitcoin a gigantic pump-and-dump, driven by early adopter cashing out at the expense of newer investors.

"I still think it's going to zero, what I am underestimating was the gullibility of the public and the marketing savvy of those promoting it."

Schiff also claims that the world is entering another "debt crisis", one that would surpass the financial crisis in 2008. He claimed that this debt crisis would not only cause hyperinflation, but it will also trigger the collapse of US treasury bonds and gold prices will soar beyond $4,000 per ounce. The dollar’s dominance, he said, is ending — with the world inevitably returning to a gold-based monetary system.

Gold’s Rough Week Adds to the Irony

Gold’s market performance, however, seems to undercut Schiff’s timing. The metal lost about 8% in just two days — a staggering $2.5 trillion wipeout in total market cap, according to The Kobeissi Letter. The sell-off followed a rapid rally earlier in the year that saw prices climb 60% amid inflation fears and global instability.

Gold was shining like never before… until it wasn’t. ✨

In its biggest one-day fall since 2013, spot gold just crashed 6.3%, wiping out weeks of euphoria in a single session.

Even the “safe haven” isn’t safe when greed peaks.
Markets have a brutal way of testing conviction,… pic.twitter.com/jNwvS2WZoY

— Ashish Kumar Meher (@AshishMeher7) October 21, 2025

Despite Schiff’s insistence that the drop is temporary, the moment underscores how volatile even “safe haven” assets can be. For many investors, that volatility only reinforces the case for Bitcoin — an asset with fixed supply, global liquidity, and no reliance on vaults or custodians.

Schiff's campaign has drawn skepticism, including CZ who calls tokenised gold a "trust me bro" asset that doesn’t represent true on-chain ownership but rather something that relies on a mutual promise.

“It’s tokenizing that you trust some third party will give you gold at some later date — even after their management changes, maybe decades later, during a war.”

Peter Schiff right now: Gold is gold bro, trust me! 😭💰 CZ just called it a ‘trust me bro token’ 💀

CZ just ended tokenized gold in one tweet 💣
Peter Schiff left the chat 😂 pic.twitter.com/gSNoVfLqns

— Anu (@anu_btc) October 24, 2025

CZ’s comments highlight a broader skepticism among crypto purists toward asset-backed tokens. Unlike decentralized cryptocurrencies, tokenized commodities like Schiff’s depend entirely on the credibility of their custodians — the exact kind of middleman crypto was designed to remove.

While Schiff insists his gold system offers transparency and utility, critics say it simply rebrands centralized trust in a digital wrapper. For CZ, the idea runs counter to everything blockchain stands for: autonomy, verifiability, and self-custody.

$trust me bro
0x44441412b008cb20f06685780fd5aaf916cba63d
currently 700 k Mc@cz_binance plans is debating @PeterSchiff and is saying that Peter Schiff's tokenized gold as custodial, not truly on-chain, needing trusted third parties aka
"Trust me bro". https://t.co/FygnopmyVWpic.twitter.com/JrgbtQ4Cf3

— cope (@OnChainCope) October 23, 2025

Many in the crypto community have also pointed out the hypocracy of how a self-proclaimed “enemy of Bitcoin” is now embracing blockchain technology to modernize the very asset he claims will replace crypto — and doing so at a time when gold has suffered its steepest two-day drop since 2013.

Gold May Glitter, But Bitcoin Still Shines Brighter

Schiff’s tokenized gold experiment is more than ironic — it’s emblematic of a broader shift. Even the most hardened gold advocates are now acknowledging that blockchain technology is here to stay. But while tokenization may modernize gold, it doesn’t change its core limitation: it still relies on trust, storage, and intermediaries.

Bitcoin, by contrast, doesn’t ask for permission — or forgiveness. It runs 24/7, audits itself every ten minutes, and exists beyond borders and politics. Gold may continue to shine in vaults, but Bitcoin continues to dominate in code. And for an asset born to replace “trust me bro” promises, that might be the ultimate proof of resilience.

data.title: Federal Reserve's "Lite Account": Promoting the Integration of AI and Crypto Payments
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

The historic closed-door meeting of the Federal Reserve quietly changed the position of cryptocurrencies in the global financial system. On October 21, at the inaugural "Payment Innovation Conference" held at the Federal Reserve headquarters, Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller explicitly stated that "the DeFi industry will no longer be viewed as suspicious or ridiculed, and the Federal Reserve will actively embrace payment innovation." This "historic" meeting was unusual in that it no longer viewed cryptocurrencies as a fringe threat to the financial system, but rather as a key component of the future payment system.

What is even more striking is that the Federal Reserve has proposed the specific concept of a "lite master account" to provide basic payment services to non-bank payment companies. This means that

stablecoin issuers and crypto payment companies are expected to directly access the Federal Reserve's payment system and break away from their dependence on traditional banks.

1. From suspicion to embrace, the Federal Reserve's attitude has changed dramatically

Global payment innovation has entered a period of acceleration, with cross-border applications of stablecoins, tokenization of traditional assets, and AI-driven payment security technologies becoming industry focus. Against this backdrop, the Federal Reserve chose not to resist but to actively participate and guide innovation. This meeting marked a fundamental shift in US regulatory attitudes toward crypto assets and AI-powered payments. The Fed's shift from past caution and skepticism to current openness and acceptance demonstrates its recognition of the trend toward financial innovation. In his speech, Governor Waller elaborated on the Fed's dual role in payment innovation: serving as a convener to resolve coordination issues and operating core payment and settlement infrastructure. He emphasized that the Fed is "looking ahead and conducting practical research on tokenization, smart contracts, and the intersection of AI and payments." This shift in attitude reflects the Fed's clear understanding of the current state of the payments revolution.

II. AI and the New Financial Era of Asset Tokenization

This conference focused on four core topics: the integration of traditional finance and digital assets, stablecoin business models, the application of AI in payments, and tokenized products, outlining a clear outline of the future payment ecosystem.

In terms of AI applications, ARK Invest CEO Cathie Wood proposed that the AI-driven "agent payment system" will usher in the era of "agent commerce."

She believes that AI can not only "know" but also "execute," making autonomous financial decisions on behalf of users. Wood predicts that the tremendous productivity unleashed by AI and blockchain is expected to drive US real GDP growth to 7% over the next five years. Richard Widmann, Head of Web3 Strategy at Google Cloud, noted the natural fit between AI and stablecoins. "AI agents can't open traditional bank accounts like humans, but they can have crypto wallets. The programmability of stablecoins makes them ideal for AI-driven automated microtransactions and machine-to-machine settlements." Asset tokenization is another major focus. Traditional financial institutions are accelerating their investment in this area. By 2025, the global tokenized asset market will be worth approximately $28 billion. Despite rapid growth, it remains far smaller than the total size of the US ETF industry.

Third, "Streamlined Master Accounts" Bridging the Last Mile

The most notable policy innovation at this conference was the Federal Reserve's proposal of a "streamlined master account" concept. This initiative is expected to pave the way for non-bank payment companies to directly access the Federal Reserve's payment system, fundamentally reducing operating costs and improving efficiency. The proposed "lite account" marks a fundamental shift in the Federal Reserve's approach to fintech, particularly crypto assets, from past skepticism and scrutiny to active acceptance and integration. As Fed Governor Waller explicitly stated, the DeFi industry is no longer subject to skepticism or derision, and the Fed will "embrace disruption, not shun it." This initiative is expected to resolve the years-long struggle for companies like Custodia Bank and Kraken to obtain master accounts, and expedite the approval process for companies like Ripple that have already submitted applications. In the long run, this represents not only technological access but also an evolution in the structure of the financial system. This foreshadows a weakening of traditional banks' privileged "plumbing" role in payments, allowing fintech companies to gain direct access to the central bank, which is expected to significantly boost innovation and competition in the payments sector. For the crypto industry, this move is profoundly significant. Crypto journalist Eleanor Terrett noted that this move is significant for companies like Custodia Bank and Kraken, which have been trying to obtain a Federal Reserve master account for years, with Custodia even suing the Fed. This move may also expedite the accessibility of companies like Ripple and Anchorage, which applied this year.

IV. Opportunities and Challenges for Hong Kong and Mainland China

Participating institutions included banks, asset management firms, retail payment companies, technology companies, and crypto-native fintech companies, reflecting that distributed ledgers and crypto assets are no longer marginalized but are increasingly integrated into the architecture of the payment and financial systems.

These actions by the Federal Reserve provide strong endorsement for the further integration of traditional finance and crypto assets.

Overall, the Federal Reserve Payments Innovation Conference has charted the course for financial digitalization in Hong Kong and Mainland China. The key for Hong Kong is balancing risks with openness, while the focus for Mainland China is on innovative applications based on its own specific circumstances. As the convergence of AI and blockchain fuels the emergence of new financial infrastructure, both regions should leverage their respective strengths to better shape the future global financial landscape. For Hong Kong, on the one hand, it can consolidate its position as a financial hub. Hong Kong already has a practical foundation in asset tokenization, with HSBC Holdings completing a cross-border tokenized US dollar deposit transaction. The Federal Reserve's move will further advance the digitization of global finance. Hong Kong can seize this opportunity to actively develop the tokenization market and explore payment innovations integrated with AI, attracting more international capital and projects and consolidating its position as an international financial center. On the other hand, Hong Kong faces competitive and regulatory pressures. As an international free port, while actively introducing innovative businesses, it also faces the challenge of striking a balance between encouraging innovation and preventing and controlling financial risks. The Federal Reserve's exploration of a regulatory framework for stablecoins suggests that Hong Kong also needs to establish a corresponding regulatory system to address potential cross-border risk transmission. For mainland China, on the one hand, this can leverage the opportunity to develop its own technology. Mainland China has already established technical standards for financial authentication and payment security. This can serve as a foundation for accelerating the deep integration of blockchain and AI technologies in scenarios such as payment, identity authentication, and supply chain finance. Furthermore, the Federal Reserve's exploration provides mainland China with valuable experience in promoting technological application while managing risks. On the other hand, mainland China needs to address technological competition and gaps. The Fed's move could accelerate digital competition in global financial infrastructure. If Hong Kong and mainland China fail to keep pace, they risk falling behind in areas such as cross-border payments and digital asset pricing. Furthermore, diverging technological approaches could lead to challenges in the interconnection of diverse digital financial ecosystems in the future. This Fed meeting is just the beginning. As the "lite master account" transitions from concept to reality, allowing stablecoin issuers and crypto payment companies to directly access the Federal Reserve's payment system, structural cracks are emerging in the traditional bank-dominated financial fortress. Furthermore, the integration of AI and blockchain could push this transformation even further. Richard Widmann, Head of Web3 Strategy at Google Cloud, noted that AI agents cannot open traditional bank accounts like humans, but can own crypto wallets. Future financial transactions may no longer be led by humans, but instead be completed automatically between AI agents through crypto wallets. Standing at a historical turning point, regulators around the world must make a choice: build higher walls or embrace irreversible trends. The Federal Reserve has already made its choice.

data.title: Binance founder CZ finally pardoned by Trump and regained full freedom
data.description:
Shaw, Golden Finance 1. Trump Announces Pardon of Binance Founder CZ On October 23rd, local time in the United States, White House officials announced that US President Trump had signed an executive order pardoning convicted Binance founder Changpeng Zhao (CZ). Press Secretary Carolyn Levitt stated in a statement that Trump "exercised his constitutional authority to pardon Changpeng Zhao, who was indicted in the Biden administration's cryptocurrency crackdown." The pardon has been reviewed by White House legal counsel. Levitt stated: "These actions by the Biden administration have severely damaged the United States' reputation as a global leader in technology and innovation. The Biden administration's war on cryptocurrency is over." Changpeng Zhao previously resigned as CEO after Binance reached a $4.3 billion settlement with the US government to end a years-long investigation. Changpeng Zhao's pardon is the latest in a series of white-collar pardons granted by Trump. 2. A Review of the CZ Prosecution On November 21, 2023, CZ reached a plea agreement with the US Department of Justice, admitting to violating the US Bank Secrecy Act by failing to establish effective anti-money laundering procedures at Binance. US prosecutors accused Binance of allowing transactions that circumvented sanctions and lacking compliance controls. The US Department of Justice and CZ reached the following settlement agreement: 1. CZ personally resigned as Binance CEO and paid a $50 million fine; 2. Binance pleaded guilty and paid a fine exceeding $4 billion, making it one of the largest corporate settlements in US history; 3. Binance must undergo three years of independent compliance monitoring; 4. CZ was banned from "managing or operating" Binance for life and was prohibited from holding any executive role at Binance.US. The former CEO position was taken over by Richard Teng. Trump's pardon of CZ means he can return to Binance's management and take over Binance's core operations. On April 30, 2024, CZ was sentenced to four months in prison in a US federal court in Seattle. It is reported that CZ is the only person in US history to be sentenced to prison for a single Bank Secrecy Act charge. 3. Why Did Trump Pardon CZ? In March of this year, the Wall Street Journal published a report claiming that representatives of the Trump family had been in talks with Binance to acquire shares in Binance US, and that CZ was seeking a presidential pardon. The report, citing multiple anonymous sources, stated that negotiations had begun in 2024 and that Steve Witkoff, Trump's Middle East peace envoy and close confidant, was involved. CZ subsequently denied the allegations on social media. He stated, "The WSJ article got the facts wrong. The fact is: I have not discussed the Binance US transaction with... anyone." He also stated, "No felon would mind a pardon, especially as the only person in American history to be imprisoned for a single Bank Secrecy Act charge." In May of this year, CZ confirmed on a podcast that he had formally submitted a pardon application to the Trump administration. The prediction market has already begun betting on whether CZ will receive a pardon. Coffeezilla posted on social media that the Hyperliquid 100% win rate whale, who profited approximately $190 million by shorting the market before Trump's tweet, had previously bet on CZ receiving a presidential pardon through the associated Polymarket account "bigwinner01." Coffeezilla noted that the timing of this operation was very close to related events, suggesting insider trading. Trump personally spoke out about his pardon decision. He said: "The reason for pardoning Binance founder CZ is because he is not guilty and is a victim of persecution by the Biden administration." Trump told reporters on Thursday afternoon: "I have indeed pardoned many people, and he (CZ) was recommended by many people. You are talking about the person in the cryptocurrency field? Many people believe that he did not commit any crime." CNN reporter Collins asked Trump about the pardon. Trump first called his question "fake news" several times to show his disparagement, and then said that he had no personal relationship with CZ. “I don’t know him and I don’t believe I’ve ever met him,” Trump said, “but I’ve been told that he has a lot of support, and they say what he did wasn’t even a crime… So at the request of many very good people, I pardoned him.” 4. Reactions from all parties to the pardon of CZ CZ himself immediately took to social media to thank Trump for the pardon: “Thank you very much for today’s pardon and thank you President Trump for upholding America’s commitment to fairness, innovation, and justice. We will do everything we can to help the United States become the capital of cryptocurrency and to advance the development of Web3 around the world. Keep going.” left;">Binance co-founder He Yi responded to CZ's pardon on social media, saying: "CZ was pardoned today. This is really exciting news. Thank you to Trump's leadership and commitment to making the United States the world's cryptocurrency capital. CZ's vision will not only make Binance the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange, but will also promote the broader cryptocurrency movement. If you are full of light in your heart, the light will shine on you."

However, there have been different voices in response to CZ's pardon. According to CBNC, Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren, a senior member of the U.S. Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs, harshly criticized the pardon, calling it an example of "corruption." "First, Zhao Changpeng (CZ) pleaded guilty to criminal money laundering charges," Warren said. "Then he promoted one of Donald Trump's cryptocurrency investments and lobbied for his pardon," she said. "Today, Donald Trump fulfilled his duty and pardoned him. Congress will be held accountable if it does not stop this kind of corruption in its upcoming market structure legislation." Then-Attorney General Merrick Garland slammed Binance when CZ pleaded guilty. "Binance became the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange in part because of its crimes—and now it is paying one of the largest corporate fines in American history," Garland said at the time. Then-Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen also said at the time: "Binance turned a blind eye to its legal obligations in its pursuit of profits. Its willful failures allowed funds to flow through its platform to terrorists, cybercriminals, and child abusers." White House Press Secretary Levitt noted in a statement on Thursday that prosecutors had requested a three-year prison sentence. She said: "The Biden administration sought to sentence Mr. Zhao to three years in prison, a sentence so far outside the sentencing guidelines that even the judge said he had never heard of such a sentence in his 30-year career."

data.title: Who will succeed Powell?
data.description:
Introduction: The Federal Reserve at a Crossroads

On October 22, 2025, global financial markets held their breath as they awaited a crucial suspense: the end of Jerome Powell's term as Chairman of the Federal Reserve (Fed) on May 15, 2026. This is not only a milestone for personnel change but also a crucial test of the direction of US monetary policy, global economic stability, and the independence of the Fed. Against the backdrop of the Trump administration's return to power, the selection of a successor, adjustments to the interest rate path, and whether the Fed can continue to withstand political pressure have become the focus of heated discussion among the market, academics, and ordinary investors. As one trader on the X platform commented, "Trump's new Fed pick has voted for a 50 basis point rate cut... Do the math yourself, an extended cycle is coming." This statement ignited market expectations and concerns about a radical shift in monetary policy following the end of the "Powell era." I. The Powell Era: A Difficult Balancing Strategy from Fighting Inflation to a Soft Landing Since being appointed by Trump as Federal Reserve Chairman in 2018, Jerome Powell has navigated the dramatic fluctuations of the US economy in recent years. From the economic shutdown triggered by the COVID-19 pandemic, to the surge in inflation in 2021-2022, to the gradual cooling in inflation from 2023-2025, the Fed under Powell's leadership has demonstrated flexibility and decisiveness. His core strategy can be summarized as "data-driven": curbing inflation through interest rate hikes, followed by cautious rate cuts to protect the job market while minimizing a hard landing. 1.1 Legacy of Fighting Inflation In 2022, facing the highest inflation in 40 years (CPI once approached 9%), the Federal Reserve launched an aggressive rate hike cycle, rapidly raising the federal funds rate from near zero to 5.25%-5.50% by early 2023. This move successfully reduced core PCE inflation from 5.2% in 2022 to 2.9% in August 2025, close to the Fed's 2% target. In numerous public speeches, Powell emphasized that the Fed's dual mandate—price stability and maximum employment—is a core pillar of its policymaking. However, this process has not been without cost. Interest rate hikes have triggered a cooling of the real estate market, increased defaults on commercial real estate (CRE) loans, and increased pressure on the banking system. For example, revised employment data for 2024 indicate that the US economy has overestimated the number of new jobs added over the past two years by approximately 1 million, signaling a weakening labor market. In his October 2025 speech, Powell admitted: "The risks we face have not completely disappeared, and downward pressure on the labor market is outweighing the stickiness of inflation." 1.2 A Cautious Beginning of Rate Cuts In September 2025, the Federal Reserve initiated a cycle of rate cuts, first lowering interest rates by 25 basis points to a range of 4.50%-4.75%. The Fed plans to cut interest rates by another 25 basis points at its October 28-29 meeting, bringing the target range to 3.75%-4.00%. This approach reflects Powell's unwavering commitment to a "soft landing": easing labor market pressures while preventing a rebound in inflation due to geopolitical factors or energy price fluctuations. However, the government shutdown that began on October 1st blocked the release of key data (such as non-farm payrolls and CPI), leaving the Fed's decision-making process navigating a dark landscape. 1.3 Powell's Declaration of Independence Amid growing political pressure, Powell has consistently emphasized the Fed's independence. In September 2025, he explicitly stated, "Politics is not a factor in our decision-making. We base our policy decisions on the data and the economic outlook." This stance was a direct response to public criticism from the Trump administration. Trump has repeatedly criticized Powell for keeping interest rates "too high," calling him a "fool" and hinting at promoting a more compliant Fed chair. However, Powell's term as Fed Chairman lasts less than eight months, while his term as a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors runs until January 2028. This means that even if he steps down as Chair, he could still influence policy as a member of the Board of Governors. However, the Trump team is clearly not satisfied with this arrangement, and the selection of a successor has become a key chess piece in their efforts to reshape the Fed. Second, the Race for Succession: Trump's "Federal Reserve Reshaping Plan" As Powell's term draws to a close, the Trump administration is accelerating its efforts, attempting to shift the Fed's policy direction toward a path more aligned with its economic agenda through the appointment of a new Chair and Board of Governors. The selection team led by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has narrowed down about five popular candidates from 11 candidates and plans to submit a final list of 3-4 to Trump after Thanksgiving. The nomination process may start in January 2026. The following is an analysis of the major candidates: 2.1 Popular candidates and their policy tendencies 2.2 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.3 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.4 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.5 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.6 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.7 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.8 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 2.9 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.10 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.11 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.12 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.13 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.14 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.15 Trump’s logic of “Federal Reserve reform” 3.16 The Trump administration's succession strategy is clear: by nominating candidates loyal to its economic vision, it will gradually transform the Federal Reserve into a tool supporting "America First" policies. Hassett and Milan's backgrounds are particularly prominent. Their close ties to the White House and their preference for loose monetary policy align closely with Trump's call for lower interest rates and economic stimulus. Market sentiment on the X platform reflects this expectation, with one user bluntly stating, "New Governor Milan's 50 basis point rate cut vote is just the beginning. Trump wants a 'MAGA Fed.'" However, this strategy is not without resistance. The Federal Reserve's legal framework grants it a high degree of independence, with Senate confirmation required for the appointment of its chairman and governors. The Trump team's attempts to challenge the legality of existing governors, such as Lisa Cook, through the courts have been rejected by lower courts, and the Supreme Court will hear the case in January 2026. Furthermore, market concerns are growing that overly aggressive easing policies could lead to runaway inflation, particularly against the backdrop of Trump's planned high tariffs (expected to push up CPI by 0.5-1%). 2.3 Market and Academic Responses Prediction markets (such as Kalshi) favor Hassett (with a 48% probability) reflect investors' expectations of Trump's growing influence. However, the conservative image of Waller and Bowman has also won support from some institutional investors. Economists worry that an overly politicized Federal Reserve could undermine its credibility, leading to a weaker dollar and the risk of asset bubbles. On the X platform, an analyst commented: "The end of quantitative tightening (QT) + significant interest rate cuts = a liquidity wave. It's a feast for Bitcoin, but a nightmare for savers." III. The Fog of the Interest Rate Path: From Cautious Rate Cuts to a Potential Wave of Easing The Federal Reserve's monetary policy is at a critical turning point. The 2025 rate cut cycle has already begun, but uncertainty about the future path is exacerbated by the government shutdown, tariff shocks, and successor preferences. The following is an analysis of the interest rate path: 3.1 Current Baseline: Gradual Rate Cuts According to the latest Reuters poll, the market generally expects two 25 basis point rate cuts in 2025, bringing the federal funds rate to around 3.25% by the end of 2026, close to the neutral rate. In his recent speech, Powell stated that current policy remains restrictive (with actual interest rates above the neutral rate), but downside risks in the labor market have prompted the Fed to accelerate the pace of easing. Another 25 basis point rate cut is expected at the October 28-29 meeting, with another 25 basis point cut possible in December, for a total of 50 basis points in 2025. However, the government shutdown has resulted in the absence of key data, making decision-making uncertain. For example, the September CPI data will be released on October 24, but the non-farm payroll report has been delayed, forcing the Fed to rely more on market signals and unofficial data. Powell admitted: "We do not have a risk-free path. We must find a balance between employment and inflation." 3.2 Dovish Pressure: Moving towards a Wave of Easing? At the September meeting, Stephen Milan, the new governor appointed by Trump, supported a 50 basis point interest rate cut, advocating a cumulative 125 basis point cut by the end of 2025, bringing rates to an ultra-low level of 2.75%-3.00%. This aggressive stance aligns with Trump's call for low interest rates and could pave the way for countering tariff shocks and stimulating economic growth. Traders on the X platform were excited about this, saying, "After Powell leaves, the Fed will embark on QE mode, flooding the market with liquidity." The end of quantitative tightening (QT) also became a focus of discussion. Powell hinted that the banking system's reserves were "super ample," but liquidity was "gradually tightening." The market interprets this as a possibility that the Federal Reserve may halt QT in early 2026, or even restart quantitative easing (QE), to counter the economic volatility caused by tariffs. This expectation has driven up the prices of hard assets like gold and Bitcoin, while also raising concerns about the devaluation of the US dollar.

3.3 Risks and Challenges

Aggressive interest rate cuts are not without cost. Trump's proposed tariffs (imposing 10-20% tariffs on imported goods) could drive up inflation, offsetting the stimulative effects of rate cuts. Energy prices, volatile due to geopolitical tensions (such as in the Middle East), also pose a risk of a rebound in inflation. Hawkish Fed figures, such as Waller, advocate cautious rate cuts, warning that overly rapid easing could lead to asset bubbles and a weakening US dollar. Furthermore, rising commercial real estate loan default rates and potential risks in the banking system require the Federal Reserve to maintain vigilant regulation while easing monetary policy.

Fourth: A Test of Independence: The Battle for the Fed's Soul

The Federal Reserve's independence has been the cornerstone of its century-long history, but the Trump administration's aggressive intervention is posing unprecedented challenges. The final months of Powell's term will be a litmus test for whether the Fed can maintain its policy autonomy. 4.1 The Legal and Political Tug of War The Federal Reserve's legal framework clearly stipulates that the appointment and removal of the Chairman and Governors must be approved by the Senate, and they cannot be arbitrarily removed due to policy differences. The Trump team attempted to challenge the legitimacy of existing Governors through the courts (such as the Lisa Cook case), but lower courts have rejected such appeals. The Supreme Court's ruling (January 2026) will be a critical turning point. Powell himself has made it clear that even if he steps down as Chairman, he will continue to serve as a Governor until 2028, preserving a certain degree of policy continuity within the Federal Reserve. 4.2 Trump's Long-Term Impact Even if Trump cannot directly replace Powell, his strategy of gradually "reshaping" the Fed through newly appointed chairmen and governors could be more far-reaching. The rise of candidates like Hassett and Milan could shift the Fed's policy focus from a dual mandate to a more singular growth orientation. This shift could boost the stock market and cryptocurrencies in the short term, but long-term risks include runaway inflation, dollar depreciation, and unstable international capital flows. 4.3 Global Perspective: The Role of the Federal Reserve The Federal Reserve's policies not only affect the US economy but also have a profound impact on global markets. In 2025, the global economy faces multiple challenges: sluggish growth in the Eurozone, a continued downturn in China's real estate market, and rising debt pressures in emerging markets. The Fed's interest rate cuts and potential quantitative easing (QE) may provide breathing space for global liquidity, but excessively loose policies could trigger currency depreciation and capital outflows from emerging markets. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) recently warned that a compromised Fed independence could undermine global confidence in the US dollar system. V. Conclusion: Unresolved suspense, ongoing gamble The suspense surrounding the end of Powell's term concerns more than just a personnel change; it also represents a gamble regarding the future direction of the Federal Reserve. The choice of his successor will determine whether monetary policy will continue Powell's cautious and balanced approach or shift toward the aggressive easing favored by Trump. The uncertainty surrounding the interest rate path, the potential impact of tariffs, and the test of the Fed's independence all lie at the heart of this high-stakes gamble. Market expectations for 2025-2026 are already divided: Optimists anticipate an asset boom fueled by a wave of liquidity, while pessimists worry about a rebound in inflation and a dollar crisis. The heated discussion on the X platform reflects this split: "After Powell's departure, the Fed will either become an engine of growth or ignite an inflation bomb." For investors, hard assets like gold and Bitcoin may be an option to hedge against uncertainty; for ordinary people, fluctuations in the cost of living will be a more direct test.

In the coming months, the September CPI data (released on October 24), the results of the October FOMC meeting, and the progress of Trump's team's nominations will provide more clues to this game. Regardless of the outcome, the battle for the Fed's soul has begun, and its outcome will profoundly shape the future of the US and even the global economy.

data.title: Trump pardons Binance founder CZ Kraken plans to go public in Q1 next year
data.description:
Headlines: US President Trump Pardons Binance Founder CZ According to the Wall Street Journal, White House officials announced Thursday that US President Trump has pardoned convicted Binance founder Changpeng Zhao (CZ). White House Press Secretary Carolyn Levitt stated in a statement that Trump "exercised his constitutional authority to pardon Changpeng Zhao, who was indicted in the Biden administration's cryptocurrency crackdown." Zhao had previously resigned as CEO of Binance after the company reached a $4.3 billion settlement with the US government, ending a multi-year investigation. Zhao's pardon marks the latest in a series of white-collar pardons granted by Trump. Earlier this year, Trump pardoned the co-founder of the BitMEX exchange, who was convicted of anti-money laundering violations, the founder of the electric truck company Nikola, who was convicted of fraud, and commuted the sentences of executives at the now-defunct Ozy Media. Kraken closed a $500 million funding round in September, raising its valuation to $15 billion. It plans to go public in Q1 of next year. According to Reuters, Apollo Global Management, Oppenheimer, Jane Street, and HSG, formerly Sequoia Capital China, all participated in the latest funding round for the cryptocurrency exchange Kraken, citing sources familiar with the matter. The round valued Kraken at $15 billion. Fortune magazine reported last month that Kraken had raised $500 million, but Reuters disclosed the identities of these major investors and financial industry participants for the first time. One source stated that Qube Research & Technologies, Kraken co-CEO Arjun Sethi, and Tribe Capital, the venture capital firm co-founded by Sethi, also participated in the round, paving the way for Kraken to go public in the coming months. Kraken expects to confidentially file a registration statement for its initial public offering (IPO) with the US SEC before the end of the year and plans to go public in the first quarter of next year. However, it was unclear how the U.S. government shutdown might affect that timeline, one of the sources added.

The Japanese Financial Services Agency is promoting a financial product regulatory plan that plans to ban banks and insurance companies from selling virtual currencies, while allowing securities companies and other institutions to engage in virtual currency sales business. Japan's Financial Services Agency (FSA) has previously considered virtual currencies as investment targets and has been exploring regulatory options under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act. The FSA argues that virtual currencies are subject to significant price volatility and pose the risk of asset leakage due to cyberattacks, necessitating the protection of the interests of depositors and insurance contract holders. However, given that online securities firms and other institutions already sell virtual currencies, the FSA has tentatively decided to allow securities subsidiaries of banks or insurance companies to sell virtual currencies, citing fair competition as a key consideration. The report also states that "it is expected that the FSA will only approve banks or insurance companies holding and operating virtual currencies once risk management measures are in place." The FSA plans to submit the relevant legislative amendments to the Diet next year.

Strategy's Bitcoin Accumulation Rate Has Declined to Its Slowest Level in Five Years. Strategy, the world's largest corporate Bitcoin treasury firm, is increasing its Bitcoin holdings at its slowest pace in five years. As of this quarter, Strategy had added only 388 Bitcoins, bringing its total holdings to 640,031 at the end of the third quarter—a mere 0.1% increase. Important Economic Developments

▌JPMorgan Chase Expects the Federal Reserve to End Quantitative Tightening Next Week

JPMorgan strategists stated in a report on Wednesday that, given tightening funding conditions this week, the Federal Reserve is likely to end quantitative tightening at next week's October Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting. JPMorgan currently expects that in addition to ending balance sheet reduction at the October meeting, the Fed will also immediately initiate temporary open market operations, particularly to alleviate funding pressures on settlement dates, the end of the Canadian fiscal year, and other periods.

▌The probability of a 25 basis point Fed rate cut in October is 98.3%

According to the CME "Fed Watch": The probability of a 25 basis point Fed rate cut in October is 98.3%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 1.7%. The probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by 50 basis points in December is 93.4%. What is Agent-Based AI? Agent-based AI refers to a type of AI designed to act on its own behalf, possessing a degree of independence and decision-making capabilities. This type of AI doesn't simply process data or respond to commands; instead, it can set goals and make decisions to achieve them, often in a human-like manner. This gives the AI ​​a sense of purpose and the ability to achieve those goals with minimal human intervention. This distinguishes it from traditional AI, which typically requires human input or predefined rules to function. Agent-based AI is self-directed, meaning it can make decisions in real time based on its environment and goals. Agent-based AI works by combining advanced machine learning techniques, decision-making algorithms, and a continuous feedback loop. Think of it as a robot that learns from experience and then uses that knowledge to influence its future actions. It typically works in the following manner: 1. Goal Setting: Agent AI determines its goals based on initial programming or ongoing environmental input. It might be set to accomplish a specific task, such as optimizing a supply chain or increasing user engagement. 2. Decision Making: It then analyzes data and uses algorithms to determine the best course of action to achieve that goal. 3. Learning and Adaptation: Like all AI systems, agent AI learns from both successes and failures. It continuously adjusts its strategies and refines its decision-making process. The key here is that AI can set its own course of action based on real-time input, making it more autonomous than traditional AI systems.

data.title: Is Binance evil? The data tells an unexpected story
data.description:
Author: ltrd Compiler: Block unicorn

Anyone who has consistently profited over the long term knows that making decisions without bias or emotion is key to a sustainable trading career. You must think outside the box and constantly reassess risk-reward and the probability of adverse choices. This is why a well-structured research process is crucial for every successful trader.

But why I'm speaking to you this way—and why I'm calling this article "Is Binance Evil?"

is simple. Over the past few weeks, I've witnessed strong sentiment surrounding Binance and other exchanges. Some of the arguments against exchanges, particularly Binance, are certainly valid, but I kept seeing biased reasoning and conclusions, so I decided to conduct a simple, transparent study of one hypothesis: H₀: "Binance is evil and a negative for projects listed on the exchange." The first thing that inspired me to conduct this research was a post by Scott Phillips (I actually love your posts and your way of thinking—nothing personal here, I hope you'll forgive me). He posted a beautiful chart showing the average price performance of all cryptocurrencies in the first 300 days after Binance's listing. There's nothing wrong with the chart itself—I like the analysis—but one thing that bothered me was the statement: "Binance is a cancer to the industry." I just couldn't see the connection between the data in the chart and the conclusion. Imagine you walked into my office (as many people do every day) and told me, "Tom, look at this chart—Binance is the cancer of this industry." You better have backed up everything on your work laptop, because you're never going to touch it again. This post isn't really about Binance—it's about testing a hypothesis and figuring it out. It's about methodological integrity and convincing people that your hypothesis is valid. Before we begin, I'd like you to critique my arguments with your own analysis. That's exactly what we do in research sessions. I'm not going to get angry—I'm so used to constructive criticism that I don't even care; I just want to make sure my analysis is correct so I can learn from it. Your only goal is to go through it and point out every possible flaw in my reasoning. I'm not here to prove that Binance isn't evil. I'm just trying to verify that this hypothesis is true. When I see this type of chart, I always think: There's a random correction missing here. What does this mean? It means I want to look at random listings from other similar exchanges and then subtract those results from the Binance dataset. That's how you remove the bias. In our case, it's not actually random because we can easily calculate all the factors associated with listings on other exchanges. Typically, in high-frequency trading, you can't "calculate everything," so I call it a random correction. When you conduct research, you need to clearly state your hypothesis: I selected all products listed on Binance (spot market) starting from January 1, 2022. Why this date? Because I didn't want to introduce confirmation bias by using data from 2020-2021, as I already knew the results would be significantly skewed towards the positive and not representative of the current market. I only included USDT trading pairs. I only included products that have been traded for more than 90 days. I excluded the first day (which is why all charts start at 0). Why? This is because exchanges handle market openings differently. Some exchanges will "artificially" create a first order well below fair value, simply to make the chart appear to surge upon listing—a completely spurious phenomenon. Some exchanges announce listings long before or immediately upon listing, making it impossible to effectively isolate announcement effects. Removing the first day makes the analysis clearer and more comparable. Of course, you can also come up with your own treatment. After completing my analysis, I obtained the following results: This is the cumulative return of all tokens that met my criteria over the first 90 days after listing on Binance's spot market. What do we see? From the outset, there was immense—absolutely immense—selling pressure. After a few days, things stabilized somewhat, and then we entered a steady downtrend. Why was this? Part of the reason is due to the overall cryptocurrency market trend. On average, tokens tend to fluctuate downward after listing. Furthermore, I selected all tokens listed after January 1, 2022, right after the bull run, so the overall environment wasn't very favorable. Now, let's address my biggest concern—the lack of random corrections. To me, without random corrections, there's no real research. Even if you showed me your last 100 runs, with an average of 10.50, I couldn't judge unless I saw it compared to the overall market. Without a benchmark, there's no judgment. In this case, "overall market" should refer to other comparable exchanges—such as Coinbase and Bybit. So, in order to do this correctly, we need to do the exact same calculations for Bybit and Coinbase (under the same conditions). Let's take a look at the chart below.

As you can see, the Coinbase chart looks much worse than the Binance chart. About 20 days after listing, the expected return drops to around -25% (and the upper confidence interval is still around -20%!). After that, we see the same pattern again—a brief period of stability followed by a slow decline, just like on Binance. The situation with Bybit is slightly different. After 90 days, the expected return still drops significantly, but the initial selling pressure isn't as intense. Based on both the data and my intuition, I believe Coinbase is much more comparable to Binance than Bybit. Now, let's actually compare these exchanges to Binance. To correct for randomness, simply subtract the above results from the main analysis for Binance. The chart below shows this. Intuitively, we now have the net impact of Binance when benchmarked against each exchange (Bybit/Coinbase). You can clearly see—especially in the case of Coinbase—that Binance's impact is positive, not negative. The selling pressure on Coinbase is much greater than on Binance. Of course, once the confidence interval is taken into account, this difference is not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level—but the conclusion is still quite clear: Binance listings outperform Coinbase listings. For Bybit, we can see that it performed significantly better in the first few days after listing. However, the difference quickly increased, and while we can say that Bybit outperformed Binance in the short term, the effect was not particularly significant. After correcting for randomness, we absolutely cannot conclude that Binance is "evil" compared to other exchanges (especially Coinbase), as projects listed on Coinbase perform significantly worse. Now, let's talk about something important—one we don't discuss enough. The Curse of Being the Ultimate Goal Imagine you're communicating with a project team that hasn't yet launched. What would you expect to hear from them? The answer is almost always something like: "Our ultimate goal is to be listed on Binance (or Coinbase, or Upbit)." This statement is crucial when we talk about the impact of a Binance listing on a project. Everyone is waiting for this moment. If you're a major investor or project founder and you truly believe you'll eventually list on Binance, Coinbase, or Upbit, what incentive would you have to sell your tokens after the listing on Bybit? I'd say almost zero—except for some operating expenses that force you to sell a small portion of your tokens. This is why you see significant selling pressure on Binance and Coinbase, and almost none on Bybit (and possibly none on Bitget, KuCoin, or Gate). However, according to our methodology, even after removing the impact of the announcement date, Binance's listing performance outperforms Coinbase's. Now, the question I'd definitely ask you is: "What percentage of tokens do you estimate an average large investor or founder would want to sell after the ultimate goal listing?" We can't answer this question directly—there's no clear data available. But you should at least have an estimate in mind, think about the logic, and come up with a number. As I mentioned before, Upbit is also an "ultimate goal" exchange, and people like listing in South Korea. Unfortunately, we still see strong selling pressure after the listing date. This is almost always a terminal blow for projects – perhaps not as severe as Binance, but still significant – and you can clearly see this in the data. The chart below shows Upbit’s performance and the difference between Binance and Upbit. After 90 days, Upbit slightly outperformed Binance, but the difference is so significant that we cannot reasonably claim that Upbit is the better listing platform. In both cases, we saw strong selling pressure - which is actually completely logical if you think about it deeply. How to price liquidity? There's one thing almost no one has considered. Following its listing, Binance boasts far greater liquidity than any other exchange. Binance allows founders and investors to partially liquidate their positions as needed, or to increase their holdings more significantly when a buyback is needed (honestly, I wish this were more common). So, how should projects or investors price this significant liquidity boost? This is something that (almost) only Binance can offer—and it's absolutely something every participant in this market should be willing to pay for, directly or indirectly. We all want deep liquidity and the ability to go short or long perpetual contracts (of course, our analysis here is focused on spot exchanges, not perpetual contracts, but it's an important feature worth mentioning). A Simple Way to Test Binance's Liquidity Advantage I've been thinking about a simple way to test whether Binance's liquidity is truly superior to other exchanges without introducing significant bias. Here's what I came up with: Find tokens listed on Bybit and Coinbase. Find tokens listed on Binance, but only after they've been listed on Bybit and Coinbase (ideally, as long as possible). Compare the liquidity of Binance, Bybit, and Coinbase a few days after they've been listed on Binance. In this setup, Bybit and Coinbase have mature markets, while Binance is an emerging market. If Binance's liquidity remains significantly better than other platforms, we can confidently say that the liquidity surplus from listing on Binance is real and substantial. This chart shows the distribution of round-trip costs—the cost of executing a $100,000 market buy and a $100,000 market sell. Higher costs indicate lower liquidity. For LA, a token listed on Binance over a month after Bybit and Coinbase, we find that after five days, the round-trip cost on Binance was 184 basis points lower than on Bybit and 110 basis points lower than on Coinbase.

For ONDO, the round-trip costs between Binance and Coinbase are roughly similar - with a slight advantage in Coinbase (only 1.77 basis points, likely due to the difference in minimum tick sizes). Now let's look at the less liquid product, AXL. Here, the cost differences are enormous. For a $100,000 trade, the cost difference is 309 basis points with Bybit and 207 basis points with Coinbase. For a $20,000 trade, the cost difference is still 41 basis points and 46 basis points, respectively. From the perspective of any current or potential holder, these figures are staggering. This is obviously not the only way to approach this topic – but it is a biased starting point. If we want to delve deeper, here are some open questions (which I won’t answer now — time, as always, is limited):

  • How should we incorporate broader market movements and their relationship to listing performance?

  • How do we quantify announcement effects and incorporate them into the analysis?

  • How should we weigh individual cases? Is ONDO more significant than AXL? If so, by what metric (perhaps market capitalization)?

  • Should we make the analysis more robust — for example, by winsorizing outliers?

  • Do the results change significantly if we exclude BSC tokens from the Binance data?

We can keep asking questions like these forever — that’s the beauty of research.

There is always room for improvement, but ultimately, creativity and research ethics are more important than any specific model. Conducting nearly unbiased research will get you further than any machine learning method. It's always about your ideas, your data preparation, and your culture of reasoning.

Concluding Remarks

We are not here just to discuss research, we are here to discuss Binance.

Whether you think Binance is "evil" or "a cancer in the industry" is entirely up to you. Please examine yourself critically. Don't let bias and emotion constrain you. Because that's not where the money is.
